blocks.content:
## POV and trial readiness

Use this path during a product POV or trial when Grafana Cloud k6 and observability already reach the workload you test. The lab assumes you can query k6 results, probe or application metrics, logs, and traces in one Grafana stack. If a signal is missing on day one, close that gap using the table below before you run the spike.

| If this is missing | Start here |
| --- | --- |
| No completed Grafana Cloud k6 run yet | [Run your first k6 performance test](/docs/learning-paths/run-first-k6-test/) |
| No traces in Tempo for the service under test | [Send traces to Grafana Cloud using Alloy](/docs/learning-paths/send-traces-alloy/) |
| No application logs in Loki | [Send logs to Grafana Cloud using Alloy](/docs/learning-paths/send-logs-alloy-loki/) |
| No synthetic probe metrics in Prometheus | [Detect customer-visible outages using Synthetic Monitoring](/docs/learning-paths/detect-outages-synthetic-monitoring/) |
| Stakeholders expect a saved dashboard, not only **Explore** | [Correlate results in Grafana](/docs/grafana-cloud/testing/k6/analyze-results/correlate-results-in-grafana/) and the optional dashboard steps in the **Prepare a live metrics view** milestone |

For a typical POV narrative, you run the spike in Grafana Cloud k6, watch load on a dashboard or **Explore** layout, then walk the same UTC window through Prometheus, Loki, and Tempo.

This path stays in Grafana Cloud k6 and **Explore** for one short spike. It does not teach long soak or capacity planning, wiring new telemetry pipelines, running k6 from CI/CD, or SLO math. Full dashboard authoring stays out of scope except for the optional short-lived layout in **Prepare a live metrics view**.

You are a performance engineer at a company that runs quickpizza, a pizza ordering API. Your team ran a spike test before a major launch. The test met every threshold, but your job is to decide whether the system is actually ready.

You chain Prometheus probe metrics, Loki logs, and Tempo traces with your k6 run by reusing one investigation window in UTC.

Record your k6 test start time in UTC and keep the run window tight. Every **Explore** query for Prometheus, Loki, and Tempo uses that same time range. That is how you ensure you're looking at the same spike across k6 and the three data sources instead of mixing unrelated traffic or ambient noise.

Finish the spike and the **Explore** milestones in one sitting when you can. Coming back the next day often still works on demo stacks, but if you wait many days you may lose logs or traces to retention even though your anchor time is still valid on paper.

How this path is structured:

1. Confirm Grafana Cloud k6, `grafanacloud-k6`, Prometheus, Loki, and Tempo data paths for quickpizza (or your workload) before you run anything.
1. Align Grafana Cloud k6 metrics with application Prometheus metrics in **Explore** on one time range.
1. Prepare a live **Explore** or dashboard layout so you can watch load and traffic during the spike.
1. Run the spike test in Grafana Cloud k6, keep the live view visible, record a test start time in UTC, then review the **Test Result** view and capture the k6 numbers you need for correlation.
1. Use Prometheus for regional, system-wide latency from synthetic probes.
1. Use Loki for error class and whether errors cluster before, during, or after the VU peak.
1. Use Tempo for span-level latency and errors.
1. Write a short postmortem-style narrative and answer reflection prompts.

Before you run the spike test, confirm telemetry and access so you are not debugging configuration mid-lab:

- Open **Explore**, add a query, and select the `grafanacloud-k6` data source. Choose **Metrics** as the query type, pick your Performance **Project**, and run a query (a past **Test run** is fine if one exists). Confirm you see data or an empty-but-valid response, not a permission or configuration error. Refer to [Correlate results in Grafana](/docs/grafana-cloud/testing/k6/analyze-results/correlate-results-in-grafana/) if the menu layout differs in your stack.
- Add a query against your Prometheus-compatible data source. Try PromQL that targets quickpizza application metrics; on some Grafana Cloud demo stacks series include `namespace="k6-quickpizza"`. Substitute the namespace, job, or service labels your administrator documents. Confirm the query returns series.
- Switch to your Loki data source. Try `{service_name="QuickPizza"}` or `{service_name="quickpizza"}` because label casing varies. Confirm the query runs without a configuration error. An empty stream can be normal if the service is quiet; a datasource error is not.
- Switch to your Tempo data source. Run `{ resource.service.name = "quickpizza" }` or the resource attribute your services use. Confirm TraceQL executes. Demo stacks often ingest OTLP through collectors such as Grafana Alloy; you only need to see that traces exist for the service, not to configure collectors in this lab.
- In Grafana Cloud k6, open **Testing & synthetics**, then **Performance**, then **Projects**, and confirm you can reach the list without an access error.

Copy the table into your notes and fill the Your value column as you go. You reuse these fields in the [Postmortem narrative](/docs/learning-paths/correlate-k6-observability/synthesize-findings/) milestone.

| Signal | What to capture | Your value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| k6 | Test start time (UTC) | |
| k6 | p95 `http_req_duration` (ms) | |
| k6 | Peak VU minute (UTC or offset from start) | |
| Prometheus | Highest-latency probe region + peak latency (ms) | |
| Loki | Representative error message text or parsed field | |
| Tempo | Slowest child span name + approximate % of trace duration | |

Check your work: you can move on when you have copied the scratch pad table, you know your test start time in UTC, and you know how long you will span in **Explore** in later milestones. If anything is unclear, open the troubleshooting topics for this milestone.

In the next milestones you align Grafana Cloud k6 metrics with application Prometheus metrics, prepare a live view, run the spike test in Grafana Cloud k6, review the completed run, and capture the UTC window you use in every **Explore** step.
